county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Clay County formed 1873 from Greene and Randolph counties. Originally named Clayton County, renamed Clay in 1875. County seat is Piggott (western district); Corning serves the eastern district. Located in northeastern Arkansas bordering Missouri.
county_metadata.research_tips: Clay County records are held at the County Clerk's office in Piggott (western district) and Corning (eastern district) -- the county has had a dual-courthouse system. Records are complete from 1873. This northeast Arkansas county borders Missouri, and many families moved back and forth across the state line. Check Missouri county records (Dunklin, Butler, Ripley) for related families. The county was originally named Clayton but changed to Clay in 1875. The clay soil and agricultural focus of the region are reflected in land records. The Piggott Public Library holds local history materials. FamilySearch has microfilmed Clay County records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Clay County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Piggott holds deed books from 1873

source_profiles.survival_notes: Deed books complete from 1873; housed at County Clerk's office in Piggott

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Cleburne County formed 1883 from Independence, Van Buren, and White counties. County seat is Heber Springs. Named for Confederate General Patrick Cleburne. Greers Ferry Lake, created in 1963, is a major feature of the county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cleburne County was formed in 1883 and records at the County Clerk's office in Heber Springs are complete from formation. Greers Ferry Lake (built 1963) inundated several communities, and pre-lake community records should be checked in adjacent county records and at the Arkansas History Commission. The county is in the Ozark foothills with a subsistence farming economy in the 1800s. Small Baptist and Church of Christ congregations hold the primary church records. The Cleburne County Historical Society in Heber Springs maintains local history files. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Cemetery surveys by local societies document many rural family burial grounds.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Cleburne County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Heber Springs holds deed books from 1883

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Cleveland County formed 1873 from Bradley, Dallas, Drew, and Lincoln counties. Originally named Dorsey County; renamed Cleveland in 1885 for President Grover Cleveland. County seat is Rison.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cleveland County records at the County Clerk's office in Rison are complete from 1873. Originally named Dorsey County, it was renamed Cleveland in 1885. This south-central county was a timber and farming area. The county's relatively late formation means many earlier records are in parent counties (Bradley, Dallas, Drew, Lincoln). The Rison Public Library holds some local history materials. The Pine Bluff/Jefferson County Library System has regional materials. FamilySearch has microfilmed Cleveland County deed books and probate records. Cemetery transcriptions cover many family burial grounds scattered throughout the rural county.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Cleveland County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Rison holds deed books from 1873

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Columbia County formed 1852 from Hempstead, Lafayette, Ouachita, and Union counties. County seat is Magnolia. Home to Southern Arkansas University. The county's economy was based on cotton, timber, and later oil production.
county_metadata.research_tips: Columbia County records are at the County Clerk's office in Magnolia, with good survival from 1852. Southern Arkansas University (formerly Southern State College) in Magnolia houses regional archives. The county is in the timber and oil region of southwest Arkansas. The 1920s oil boom (Smackover field extends into northern Columbia County) brought rapid population growth documented in land leases and local newspapers. Probate records contain enslaved persons inventories from the antebellum period. The Columbia County Historical Society maintains research files. Freedmen's Bureau records are available at NARA for African American research. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Columbia County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Magnolia holds deed books from 1852

county_metadata.record_losses: 1854 courthouse fire destroyed some early deeds and court records; many deed books survived
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Conway County formed 1825 from Pulaski County. County seat is Morrilton. Named for Henry Wharton Conway, territorial delegate to Congress. The 1854 courthouse fire destroyed many early records. Located in the Arkansas River valley.
county_metadata.research_tips: Conway County's 1854 courthouse fire destroyed many early records, though some deed books survived. The County Clerk's office in Morrilton holds surviving and post-fire records. This Arkansas River valley county was a cotton-producing area with significant slaveholding. Petit Jean Mountain and the surrounding area attracted early settlers from Tennessee and Kentucky. The Morrilton Public Library holds local history collections. Check the Arkansas History Commission for microfilmed surviving records. The University of Central Arkansas in nearby Conway has regional history collections. GLO land patents document original federal land entries.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Conway County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Morrilton holds deed books from 1825

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Craighead County formed 1859 from Greene, Mississippi, and Poinsett counties. County seat is Jonesboro (western district); Lake City serves the eastern district. Home to Arkansas State University. Located on Crowley's Ridge in northeastern Arkansas.
county_metadata.research_tips: Craighead County records at the County Clerk's office in Jonesboro are well-preserved from 1859. Jonesboro has a dual courthouse system with Lake City. Arkansas State University in Jonesboro houses the Arkansas State University Heritage Sites and regional archives, making it an excellent research center. The county is in the northeast Arkansas delta region, historically a cotton and rice area. Railroad records from the 1880s-1900s document many families. The Craighead County Historical Society and the Crowley's Ridge Nature Center provide local research support. FamilySearch has indexed Craighead County vital records and microfilmed deed books.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Craighead County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Jonesboro holds deed books from 1859

county_metadata.record_losses: 1877 courthouse fire destroyed deed records and court records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Crawford County formed 1820 from Pulaski County. County seat is Van Buren, on the Arkansas River across from Fort Smith. One of the earliest counties formed in the territory. The 1877 courthouse fire destroyed deed records and court records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Crawford County's 1877 courthouse fire destroyed deed records and court records, making pre-fire research challenging. The County Clerk's office in Van Buren holds post-fire records. Van Buren, on the Arkansas River across from Fort Smith, was a significant staging point for westward migration. Crawford County was one of the earliest formed in the territory (1820). Fort Smith National Historic Site has records of the federal court for the Western District of Arkansas (Judge Parker's court). The Crawford County Historical Society maintains reconstructed records. For pre-1877 land records, check GLO patents at BLM and the Arkansas History Commission. Cherokee and Choctaw removal routes passed through Crawford County.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Crawford County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Van Buren holds deed books from 1830

county_metadata.record_losses: 1869 courthouse fire destroyed many records; additional losses from Mississippi River flooding
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Crittenden County formed 1825 from Phillips County. County seat is Marion. Located in eastern Arkansas directly across the Mississippi River from Memphis, Tennessee. The 1869 courthouse fire and Mississippi River flooding caused significant record losses.
county_metadata.research_tips: Crittenden County's 1869 courthouse fire destroyed many records, and Mississippi River flooding caused additional losses. The County Clerk's office in Marion holds surviving records. This eastern Arkansas county directly across from Memphis, Tennessee had strong economic and social ties to Memphis. Many Crittenden County families appear in Memphis/Shelby County, Tennessee records. The county's large plantation economy means enslaved persons records in estate inventories are important. Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA are essential. The West Memphis Public Library has local history materials. For pre-fire records, check the Arkansas History Commission and adjacent county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Crittenden County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Marion holds deed books from 1835

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Cross County formed 1862 from Crittenden, Poinsett, and St. Francis counties during the Civil War. County seat is Wynne. Located in eastern Arkansas in the delta region.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cross County records at the County Clerk's office in Wynne are complete from 1862. Formed during the Civil War, the county's early records reflect wartime conditions. This eastern Arkansas delta county was a rice and cotton area. The Village Creek State Park area has historical significance for the county. The Cross County Historical Society maintains local research files. Railroad expansion in the 1880s-1900s transformed the county and is documented in land records and local newspapers. Freedmen's Bureau records cover formerly enslaved families. FamilySearch has microfilmed Cross County records. Check Crittenden, Poinsett, and St. Francis county records for earlier families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Cross County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Wynne holds deed books from 1862

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Dallas County formed 1845 from Bradley, Clark, and Hot Spring counties. County seat is Fordyce. Named for George Mifflin Dallas, vice president under James K. Polk. Located in the timber belt of south-central Arkansas.
county_metadata.research_tips: Dallas County records are at the County Clerk's office in Fordyce, with good survival from 1845. The county is in the timber belt of south-central Arkansas. The Fordyce Lumber Company and other timber operations brought workers from across the South; company records and payroll ledgers may survive in private collections. Probate records contain enslaved persons inventories. The Fordyce Public Library has local history materials. The county was named for George Mifflin Dallas, vice president under James K. Polk. FamilySearch has microfilmed Dallas County records. GLO land patents document original federal land entries in this public land state.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Dallas County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Fordyce holds deed books from 1845

source_profiles.source_name: Dallas County Deed Books -- County Clerk / Arkansas History Commission
source_profiles.repository: Dallas County Clerk, Fordyce / Arkansas History Commission, Little Rock
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books for Dallas County recording land conveyances, mortgages, and property transfers; County Clerk's office is the primary repository; Arkansas History Commission holds microfilm copies
source_profiles.survival_notes: Deed books complete from 1845; housed at County Clerk's office in Fordyce

source_profiles.source_name: Dallas County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census records for Dallas County; slave schedules document the significant slaveholding in southwest Arkansas

source_profiles.source_name: Dallas County Probate Records -- County Clerk / Arkansas History Commission
source_profiles.repository: Dallas County Clerk, Fordyce / Arkansas History Commission, Little Rock
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, inventories, administrations, and estate records for Dallas County; estate inventories frequently list enslaved persons by name, age, and value -- essential for African American genealogy

source_profiles.source_name: Dallas County Marriage Records -- County Clerk / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: Dallas County Clerk, Fordyce / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records for Dallas County including bonds, licenses, and certificates; County Clerk's office is the repository

source_profiles.source_name: Dallas County Federal Land Patents -- BLM / GLO
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents (original entries) for Dallas County from the General Land Office; Arkansas is a public land state and most original land titles derive from federal patents; searchable by county and patentee name at BLM website

source_profiles.source_name: Dallas County Freedmen's Bureau Records -- NARA / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Freedmen's Bureau records for Dallas County including labor contracts, marriage records, school reports, and complaints; essential for tracing African American families in the transition from slavery to freedom
